original_hypothesis.title: TradeHelper.getAmountsIn Infinite Loop Due to uint Underflow in Decrementing Loop Counter
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: getAmountsIn uses `for (uint i = routes.length-1; i >= 0; i--)` where i is uint256. When i reaches 0, the decrement i-- causes underflow to type(uint256).max (Solidity 0.8.13 unchecked in for-loop post-condition). The condition `i >= 0` is always true for unsigned integers, creating an infinite loop that exhausts all gas.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a well-known pattern bug with unsigned integer decrementing loops. The function is completely non-functional for any input, rendering reverse-path amount calculations impossible for all integrators.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint i = routes.length-1; i >= 0; i--) -- uint underflow when i=0, i-- wraps to max; i >= 0 always true

preconditions: Any call to getAmountsIn with valid routes

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Loop never terminates; always consumes all gas
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use explicit break condition or different loop structure

analysis_questions: Is getAmountsIn called by any on-chain contract in the protocol?
Are there off-chain integrators relying on this function?
Does this block any critical protocol operation?
validation_requirements: Call getAmountsIn with any valid route and confirm out-of-gas revert
Verify uint decrement behavior at i=0 in Solidity 0.8.13

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Any call to getAmountsIn always reverts with out-of-gas. Function is completely non-functional.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Gas loss on every call; broken reverse routing for integrators

validation_result.mechanism: uint underflow revert in decrementing loop
validation_result.impact_summary: TradeHelper.getAmountsIn is completely non-functional — it always reverts after processing the last valid iteration (i=0) due to uint256 underflow in the post-decrement expression `i--`, making reverse-path amount calculations impossible for all callers.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis correctly identifies that `getAmountsIn` has a fatal bug in its loop structure `for (uint i = routes.length-1; i >= 0; i--)`. However, the exact failure mechanism differs slightly from the hypothesis in Solidity 0.8.13:

1. The condition `i >= 0` is indeed always true for uint256 — this is correct.
2. In Solidity 0.8.13, arithmetic overflow/underflow checks are enabled BY DEFAULT (not unchecked). The for-loop post-expression `i--` is NOT in an unchecked block.
3. Therefore, when i=0, the post-expression `i--` triggers a Panic(0x11) arithmetic underflow revert — it does NOT wrap to type(uint256).max.
4. The function successfully processes all iterations from routes.length-1 down to 0, then REVERTS on the `i--` after the i=0 iteration body completes.

The net result is identical to the hypothesis's conclusion: the function is completely non-functional and always reverts. The difference is the revert reason (Panic vs out-of-gas). The code defect is real — the loop structure is fundamentally broken for unsigned integers in Solidity 0.8.x.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: TradeHelper.sol lines 112-117 contain the exact pattern described. The `for (uint i = routes.length-1; i >= 0; i--)` loop will always revert because: (a) `i >= 0` is tautologically true for uint256, and (b) `i--` at i=0 triggers an arithmetic underflow panic in Solidity 0.8.13. The pragma is confirmed as `pragma solidity 0.8.13;` at the top of the file.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The loop uses `uint i` (uint256) with a decrementing pattern and condition `i >= 0`. Since uint256 is always >= 0, the condition never terminates the loop. In Solidity 0.8.13 (with checked arithmetic), when i reaches 0, the post-expression `i--` causes a Panic(0x11) underflow revert. This means the function processes all valid iterations but then always reverts, discarding all computed results. The function is completely non-functional.

calling_sequence: 2. amounts array created with length 2, amounts[1] = amountOut
5. Loop body: (amounts[0],) = getAmountIn(amounts[1], routes[0].from, routes[0].to) — executes successfully
6. Loop post: i-- → 0 - 1 → Panic(0x11) arithmetic underflow REVERT
7. All state changes (amounts array) are discarded, function reverts
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Any call to getAmountsIn with any valid routes array will follow the same pattern: the loop processes all indices from routes.length-1 down to 0 correctly, but then the post-decrement at i=0 causes a Solidity 0.8.x arithmetic underflow panic, reverting the entire call. The function is called by GlobalRouter.getAmountInStable (indirectly via getAmountIn), making the GlobalRouter's reverse quoting functionality also broken.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for TradeHelper. The hardhat.config.js confirms Solidity 0.8.13 compiler with optimizer enabled.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack scenario per se — it is a code defect that renders getAmountsIn completely non-functional. Any caller (on-chain contract or off-chain script) that invokes TradeHelper.getAmountsIn will receive a revert. The GlobalRouter contract exposes getAmountInStable which transitively depends on TradeHelper. No reverse-path amount calculation is possible through TradeHelper. The impact is limited because: (1) it's a view function so no state is corrupted, (2) callers only lose gas, and (3) the forward-path functions (getAmountsOut) work correctly.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The function getAmountsIn is completely broken and always reverts. This breaks reverse-path amount calculations for any integrator using TradeHelper or GlobalRouter. However, the impact is mitigated because: (1) it is a view/pure function — no funds are at risk, (2) forward-path quoting via getAmountsOut works normally, (3) RouterV2 does not use this function, and (4) callers only lose gas on failed calls. The defect would be critical if any on-chain protocol relied on getAmountsIn for swap execution, but it appears to be primarily used for quoting.
recommended_mitigations: Replace the loop with a safe pattern: `for (uint i = routes.length; i > 0; i--) { uint idx = i - 1; (amounts[idx],) = getAmountIn(amounts[idx+1], routes[idx].from, routes[idx].to); }`
Alternatively, use `if (i == 0) break;` at the end of the loop body before the decrement
Add a unit test that calls getAmountsIn with valid routes to verify it returns without reverting
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy TradeHelper with a valid PairFactory address. 2. Create at least one pair. 3. Call getAmountsIn with amountOut=1e18 and a single-element routes array pointing to the created pair. 4. Observe that the call reverts with Panic(0x11). 5. Verify by wrapping in try/catch that it always reverts regardless of input parameters.
uncertainties: The exact revert mechanism is Panic(0x11) arithmetic underflow, not an infinite loop/OOG as the hypothesis states. The hypothesis's characterization of 'Solidity 0.8.13 unchecked in for-loop post-condition' is incorrect — Solidity 0.8.x DOES check arithmetic in for-loop post-expressions by default.

It is unclear whether any on-chain contract in production actually calls getAmountsIn. GlobalRouter exposes getAmountInStable which calls getAmountIn (not getAmountsIn directly). The actual on-chain impact depends on integrator usage.
additional_testing_needed: Verify with a Hardhat test that getAmountsIn always reverts for any valid input
Check if any deployed contract or frontend relies on getAmountsIn
Confirm the revert reason is Panic(0x11) and not OOG by examining the revert data
